Title: [i18n:packs] Create Sinhala (si) Python language pack using Tiny Aya translation model
Description:
Create the Sinhala (si) Python language pack for use in the Expedition Tiny Aya hackathon.

**Steps:**

1. Create directory: `libs/i18n/language-packs/python/si/`
2. Follow structure of existing packages (e.g., `ar/` or `es/`)
3. Create translation YAML files for each Python version (3.10-3.14)
4. Add package metadata: `pyproject.toml`, `CHANGELOG.md`, `README.md`, `__init__.py`
5. Update root `pyproject.toml` (uv.sources + workspace members)
6. Run `uv sync` to install in development mode
7. Validate with `legesher-i18n validate`

**Sinhala details:**

* ISO 639-1: `si`
* Script: Sinhala
* RTL: No

**Note:** Sinhala is NOT supported by Tiny Aya, but the language pack is still useful for Legesher's broader language coverage.

**Blocking:** Stress test (CORE-1000) depends on this pack being created.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [i18n:packs] Create Urdu (ur) Python language pack using Tiny Aya translation model

Create a new Urdu (ur) Python language pack for Legesher. Urdu uses Nastaliq script (RTL).

Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [core:research] The Stack Dataset — Non-English Content Analysis for Expedition Tiny Aya
Description:
## Overview

Research and analysis of non-English content in Hugging Face's The Stack dataset to support the Expedition Tiny Aya proposal (CORE-1078). This work establishes the empirical foundation for why multilingual code datasets don't exist and why Legesher is uniquely positioned to create them.

## Key Findings

### The Stack Dataset (Hugging Face)

* **The Stack v1**: 6.4 TB, 358 programming languages — all with English keywords
* **The Stack v2**: 619 programming languages — actively filtered out non-English Markdown from PRs
* **Python subset**: \~96% of comments/docstrings are English, \~4% across \~40 detected languages
* **GitHub Issues subset**: 54 GB / 31M rows, described as "mostly in English"
* **Critical gap**: No public dataset contains code where keywords are non-English

### Stanford Study — "Human Languages in Source Code" (2019)

* Analyzed 2.9M Java repos from 1.1M GitHub users
* 12.7% of GitHub users write non-English commit messages
* 93% of Chinese projects have ASCII-only identifiers, yet 99% have non-ASCII comments
* Only 0.6% of Arabic/Hebrew repos have native-script identifiers (1 file with 1 Arabic identifier)
* Proves developers want native-language coding but are blocked by tooling

### Non-English Programming Languages

* **1C:Enterprise** (Russian): 5M users, 80-90% of Russian companies — proves non-English code works at enterprise scale
* **Wenyan** (Classical Chinese): 20.1k GitHub stars
* **Qalb** (Arabic): Academic project demonstrating RTL programming
* None exist for open-source Python — Legesher fills this gap

## Documents Created

* `research/proposal.md` — Updated PRD with Section 1.5 (Background & Prior Work)
* `research/stack-dataset-non-english-analysis.md` — Standalone research brief (shareable)
* `research/analyze_stack_languages.py` — Python script to run language detection on The Stack
* `research/requirements.txt` — Script dependencies

## Sources

 1. [The Stack v1](<https://huggingface.co/datasets/bigcode/the-stack>) — Kocetkov et al., [arXiv:2211.15533](<https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.15533>), 2022
 2. [The Stack v2](<https://huggingface.co/datasets/bigcode/the-stack-v2>) — Lozhkov et al., [arXiv:2402.19173](<https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.19173>), 2024
 3. [Human Languages in Source Code](<https://arxiv.org/abs/1909.04556>) — Piech et al., Stanford, 2019 ([ACM L@S 2020](<https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3386527.3405916>))
 4. [The Stack GitHub Issues](<https://huggingface.co/datasets/bigcode/the-stack-github-issues>) — 31M rows
 5. [The Stack Metadata](<https://huggingface.co/datasets/bigcode/the-stack-metadata>)
 6. [BigCode Analysis Repo](<https://github.com/bigcode-project/bigcode-analysis>) — comment extraction tools
 7. [1C:Enterprise](<https://1csoftware.com/>) / [Wikipedia](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1C:Enterprise>)
 8. [Wenyan Lang](<https://github.com/wenyan-lang/wenyan>) — Classical Chinese PL
 9. [Qalb](<https://github.com/nasser/--->) — Arabic PL
10. [Non-English PLs — Wikipedia](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-English-based_programming_languages>)
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [core:research] Expedition Tiny Aya — "Does Multilingual Code Improve Multilingual Models?"
Description:
## Overview

Research proposal for Cohere Labs' **Expedition Tiny Aya** program (March 10 – April 23, 2026). Testing whether multilingual code — Python transpiled into non-English languages via Legesher — improves Tiny Aya's performance on non-code tasks (reasoning, world knowledge, instruction following) in those languages.

**Core question:** If English code makes models better at English reasoning (\~8% improvement per Cohere's "To Code or Not to Code" 2024 paper), does multilingual code do the same for other languages?

**Partners:** Code Division, Naamal
**Team size:** 5–7 people
**Timeline:** March 10 – April 23, 2026 (2-week hackathon + 4-week publication phase)

## What We Ship

* Open multilingual code datasets on HuggingFace (first of their kind)
* Controlled experiment results across 3+ languages and 4 training conditions
* Regional variant analysis (Earth/Fire/Water vs. Global Tiny Aya models)
* Blog post or paper extending Cohere's published work

## Target Languages

* **Arabic (ar)** — Semitic, RTL, root-based morphology ✅ Pack exists (94.5% coverage)
* **Hindi (hi)** — Indo-Aryan, Devanagari, SOV word order ✅ Pack exists (95.6% coverage)
* **Third language TBD** — ⚠️ Swahili does NOT exist in Legesher. Candidates: Turkish (tr), Korean (ko), Chinese (zh)

## Training Conditions (4 conditions × base + regional variants)

1. **Baseline** — Tiny Aya base, no fine-tuning
2. **English code** — LoRA fine-tune on English Python code from The Stack
3. **Multilingual code** — LoRA fine-tune on Legesher-transpiled Python code (per language)
4. **Multilingual NL text** — LoRA fine-tune on equivalent natural language text (control)

## Key Decision Points

| When | Decision |
| -- | -- |
| Pre-hackathon (stress test) | Is Legesher ready for batch? ≥90% success → proceed |
| Day 5 (data handoff) | Any language below 80% success rate? |
| Day 8 (first results) | Does multilingual code outperform baseline? |
| Day 15 (hackathon wrap) | What's the publication story? |

## Legesher Readiness Assessment

See attached document for full analysis. Key findings:

* ✅ Core transpiler is production-ready (\~140μs/file)
* ✅ Arabic and Hindi language packs exist with 94-96% coverage
* ⚠️ No batch processing infrastructure (sequential only, no parallelization)
* ⚠️ No HuggingFace Datasets integration (`datasets` library not in dependencies)
* ❌ Swahili language pack does not exist — must substitute
* ❌ 1,000-file stress test on The Stack not yet run

## Weekend Priorities (March 7-9)

- [ ] Run 1,000-file stress test from The Stack
- [ ] Choose third language (not Swahili)
- [ ] Sharpen research question (pre-training vs fine-tuning framing)
- [ ] Generate 50-100 sample transpiled files per language for champion review
- [ ] Prototype batch processing wrapper

## Related Issues

* CORE-648: CohereForAI partnership exploration
* CORE-513: Cohere LLM research
* CORE-640: HuggingFace organization setup
* CORE-487: Phase 4 custom translation model
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [core:tree-sitter] UTF-8 encoding error on files with non-UTF-8 bytes in string literals
Description:
## Context

Discovered during CORE-685 stress test (10-file smoke test against The Stack dataset).

## Problem

TreeSitterTranslator.translate_code() throws InvalidCodeError on Python files that contain non-UTF-8 bytes (e.g., 0x9f) inside string literals or comments. The same files translate successfully with TokenTranslator.

Error: Failed to translate code: utf-8 codec can not decode byte 0x9f in position 894: invalid start byte

Tree-sitter is a C library that processes raw bytes. When it reconstructs the source after AST manipulation, it re-encodes the byte buffer and chokes on bytes that are not valid UTF-8, even though Python ast.parse() accepts them.

## Impact

* 1/10 files (10%) in smoke test. Need 1000-file run for real error rate.
* Token backend works as fallback, so not a blocker for Tiny Aya, but limits tree-sitter reliability at scale.

## Suggested fixes

1. Pre-filter: Validate all bytes are valid UTF-8 before passing to tree-sitter, fallback to token backend if not
2. Sanitize: Replace non-UTF-8 bytes in non-code regions before tree-sitter processing, restore after
3. Catch and fallback: Catch encoding error in TreeSitterTranslator and fall back to TokenTranslator automatically

## Related

* CORE-685: Stress test that surfaced this
* libs/core/legesher_core/tree_sitter/translator.py
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:native] Design coding exercises for native contributors — 3 tiers (basic, applied, domain-specific)
Description:
Design a set of coding exercises that native-language developers can complete using Legesher.

**3 Tiers:**

**Tier 1: Basic (10-20 lines)**

* FizzBuzz, factorial, palindrome check, list sorting
* Tests fundamental keyword usage

**Tier 2: Applied (20-50 lines)**

* File parser, simple API client, data transformer, calculator
* Tests natural problem decomposition in native language

**Tier 3: Domain-specific (50-100 lines)**

* Data analysis script, web scraper, simple game, algorithm implementation
* Tests whether native-language developers structure code differently

**Per exercise:**

* Clear problem statement (in target language)
* Expected input/output examples
* Difficulty level
* Estimated completion time

**Deliverable:** Exercise set document (in English + translations for zh, si, ur)
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Download all evaluation benchmarks and verify they work for the target languages before the hackathon.

**Benchmarks:**

**Note:** Amharic benchmark availability may be more limited than Chinese/Urdu. Document which benchmarks have am subsets and identify alternatives if needed.

2. Verify target language subsets exist and are usable

Title: [research:infra] Pool Kaggle GPU access — collect accounts from 5-7 crew, document T4 hour budget (300-420 hrs target)

Pool Kaggle free tier GPU access across the team to maximize available compute hours.

6. Document how to share notebooks/data across accounts

**Budget estimate:** 5 accounts × 30 hrs/week × 2 weeks = 300 hrs; 7 accounts = 420 hrs
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:infra] Set up experiment tracking — W&B project or HuggingFace hub for training run logging
Description:
Set up centralized experiment tracking so all training runs are logged consistently.

**Options (pick one):**

* Weights & Biases (W&B) project — better visualizations, free for academics
* HuggingFace Hub — simpler, integrates with transformers trainer

**Requirements:**

1. All team members can log runs

Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:pre-hack] Review Legesher keyword mappings for Chinese, Amharic, and Urdu — flag issues
Description:
Language champions review the keyword and builtin translations for all three target languages before training data is generated.

**Review checklist per language:**

* Are keyword translations natural and idiomatic?
* Are there ambiguous translations that could confuse developers?
* Are builtins translated consistently?
* For RTL languages (Urdu): do translations read correctly in RTL context?
* For CJK (Chinese): are translations appropriately concise?
* For Ge'ez script (Amharic): are translations natural in Ethiopic script?

**Deliverable:** Review report per language with flagged issues and suggested corrections.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:native] Define "native code" criteria — what qualifies, what doesn't, quality rubric
Description:
Define clear criteria for what constitutes "native code" for this research.

**Native code = code written by developers who think in the target language**, using Legesher to write Python with native-language keywords.

**Qualification criteria:**

1. Written by a developer who is a native/fluent speaker of the target language
2. Written from scratch (not transpiled from English)
3. Developer used Legesher to write with native-language keywords
4. Code demonstrates natural problem-solving patterns (not translated English logic)

**Disqualification:**

* Code translated word-by-word from an English solution
* Auto-generated code
* Copy-pasted code with keywords swapped

**Quality rubric (1-5 scale):**

1. Clearly translated from English
2. Mostly English-logic with native keywords
3. Mix of native and English patterns
4. Mostly native thinking patterns
5. Fully native — reflects how a native speaker would approach the problem

**Deliverable:** Written criteria document, shareable with contributors and reviewers.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:pre-hack] Set up training environment — test QLoRA config on Tiny Aya with dummy data
Description:
Set up the QLoRA fine-tuning environment and validate it works before the hackathon starts.

**Steps:**

1. Install dependencies: transformers, peft, bitsandbytes, datasets, accelerate
2. Download Tiny Aya base model (3.35B params)
3. Configure QLoRA 4-bit quantization (\~5.4GB VRAM, fits on T4)
4. Run a test fine-tune on dummy data (100 examples, 1 epoch) to verify pipeline works end-to-end
5. Document the exact environment setup, dependencies, and config for the team
6. Test on Kaggle T4 notebook to confirm it works within free tier

**Success criteria:** Complete training loop runs without errors, adapter saves correctly, inference works with merged adapter.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:infra] Set up shared HuggingFace organization — repos for datasets, models, and experiment tracking
Description:
Create a shared HuggingFace organization for the Expedition Tiny Aya team.

**Repos to create:**

* Dataset repos (one per language per condition)

2. Invite all 7 team members
3. Set up repo templates for datasets and models

5. Document organization structure and naming conventions

Title: [i18n:packs] Create Amharic (am) Python language pack using Tiny Aya translation model
Description:
Create the Amharic (am) Python language pack for use in the Expedition Tiny Aya hackathon.

**Why Amharic:** Amharic is supported by Tiny Aya and maps to the **Earth** regional variant (Africa/West Asia). Combined with Chinese (zh → Water) and Urdu (ur → Fire), this gives us coverage across all 3 Tiny Aya regional variants.

**Steps:**

1. Create directory: `libs/i18n/language-packs/python/am/`
2. Follow structure of existing packages (e.g., `ar/` or `es/`)
3. Create translation YAML files for each Python version (3.10-3.14)
4. Add package metadata: `pyproject.toml`, `CHANGELOG.md`, `README.md`, `__init__.py`
5. Update root `pyproject.toml` (uv.sources + workspace members)
6. Run `uv sync` to install in development mode
7. Validate with `legesher-i18n validate`

**Amharic details:**

* ISO 639-1: `am`
* Script: Ge'ez (Ethiopic)
* RTL: No
* Tiny Aya variant: Earth (Africa/West Asia)

**Blocking:** Stress test and bulk transpilation depend on this pack being created.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:data] Filter and clean NL text to match domain distribution of transpiled code
Description:
Filter and clean the NL text corpora to create a fair control condition.

**Filters:**

1. Language verification (remove misclassified text)
2. Quality filters (min length, remove boilerplate, deduplicate)
3. Domain matching: try to match the domain distribution of code-adjacent text (technical, documentation-like) rather than random web crawl
4. Remove offensive/inappropriate content
5. UTF-8 validation

**Output:** Clean NL text corpora per language, ready for tokenization.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:eval] Build evaluation pipeline — run benchmarks per adapter per language automatically
Description:
Build an automated evaluation pipeline that can run all benchmarks for any adapter on any language.

**Pipeline requirements:**

1. Input: adapter path (HF hub or local), language code, benchmark list
2. Load base model + merge adapter
3. Run each benchmark and collect scores
4. Output: JSON results file with all metrics
5. Support batch mode: evaluate multiple adapters in sequence
6. Logging: track compute time per benchmark

**Benchmarks to support:**

* XNLI (NLI accuracy)
* XStoryCloze (commonsense accuracy)
* TyDi QA (F1, EM)
* MMLU (accuracy per subject)
* MultiNRC, AI4Math (added later)

**Deliverable:** Reusable evaluation script that any team member can run.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:review] Review 50-file transpilation sample for Chinese (zh) — quality check and feedback
Description:
Language champion reviews a 50-file sample of Chinese transpiled code for quality.

**Review criteria:**

* Are keywords translated correctly and naturally?
* Does the transpiled code read logically in Chinese?
* Are there awkward or confusing translations?
* Do variable names / string literals remain correct?
* Are comments handled appropriately?

**Process:**

1. Sample 50 files from the transpiled Chinese corpus
2. Review each file, annotating issues
3. Categorize issues: critical (wrong translation), moderate (awkward), minor (style)
4. Provide feedback to pack maintainer for corrections
5. Estimate overall quality score (1-5)

**Deliverable:** Review report with annotated issues and quality score.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:native] Coordinate with Code Division / Naamal — recruit 3-5 developers per language
Description:
Reach out to Code Division and Naamal to recruit native-language developers.

**Target:** 3-5 developers per language (zh, si, ur) = 9-15 total contributors

**Code Division:** Organization focused on inclusive tech education
**Naamal:** Organization working with South Asian developer communities

**Outreach steps:**

1. Prepare partnership pitch (share contributor guide + exercise set)
2. Contact organizations, explain research goals
3. Ask them to recruit developers from their communities
4. Provide Legesher installation support if needed
5. Set up communication channel for contributors (Discord?)

**Deliverable:** Confirmed partnerships with committed developer counts per language.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:infra] Create shared documentation hub — access matrix, role assignments, links to all resources
Description:
Create a single source of truth document that every team member can reference.

**Contents:**

1. **Team roster:** Name, role, timezone, contact info
2. **Access matrix:** Who has access to what (HuggingFace, Kaggle, W&B, GitHub, Linear)

**Format:** Notion page, Google Doc, or HackMD — whatever the team prefers.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:eval] Evaluate Conditions 1-2 adapters — first results checkpoint (Day 8)
Description:
Run evaluation on the first two trained adapters to get initial signal on the hypothesis.

**Evaluate:**

* Condition 1 adapter (English code) on all benchmarks for zh/si/ur
* Condition 2 adapter (multilingual transpiled code) on all benchmarks for zh/si/ur

**Key question:** Does Condition 2 outperform Condition 1 on target-language benchmarks? This is the first signal on whether multilingual code matters.

**Deliverable:** Comparison table + brief analysis for midpoint sync (#62).

**This is the Day 8 checkpoint** — results inform whether to adjust the remaining training schedule.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:pipeline] Build batch processing wrapper with error logging, parallelization, retry logic
Description:
Build the batch processing infrastructure for running Legesher transpilation at scale.

**Requirements:**

1. Multiprocessing pool (configurable worker count)
2. Per-file error logging to CSV (file path, error type, traceback)
3. Retry logic for transient failures (max 3 retries)
4. Progress bar with ETA
5. Checkpoint file for resume after interruption
6. Memory-efficient: process files in batches, don't load all into memory

**Integration:** Works with both the HuggingFace streaming source (#19) and local filtered files (#20).

**Target throughput:** >100 files/sec on consumer hardware.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:data] Run bulk transpilation — Chinese (zh), Amharic (am), Urdu (ur) — 50K+ files each
Description:
Execute the bulk transpilation pipeline for all three target languages.

**Per language:**

* Source: 50K-100K Python files from The Stack (filtered subset)
* Transpile: en → zh, en → am, en → ur using Legesher token backend
* Output: transpiled Python files + metadata
* Track: success rate, round-trip accuracy, processing time

**Depends on:**

* Batch transpilation wrapper (CORE-976)
* Filtered Stack subset from Pipeline Engineer (CORE-882, CORE-872)
* Language packs: CORE-1128 (am), CORE-1071 (ur), zh (existing)

**Order:** zh first (existing pack, most data), then am and ur as packs are validated.

**Deliverable:** 3 transpiled corpora (50K+ files each) ready for packaging as HuggingFace Datasets.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:pipeline] Package transpiled corpora as HuggingFace Datasets with metadata
Description:
Package the transpiled code into HuggingFace Dataset format for training.

**Per dataset:**

* Columns: code (transpiled), code_en (original), language, file_path, license, token_count
* Split: train (90%), validation (10%)
* Format: Parquet files for efficient loading
* One dataset per language (zh, si, ur)

**Steps:**

1. Convert transpiled files to HuggingFace Dataset objects
2. Add metadata columns
3. Tokenize with Tiny Aya tokenizer, record token counts
4. Create train/validation splits
5. Save as Parquet
6. Validate: load dataset, check schema, verify sample quality
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:training] Finalize LoRA training configs for all conditions + set up experiment tracking (W&B or HF)
Description:
Create and validate the QLoRA training configurations that will be used across all experimental conditions.

**Config parameters to finalize:**

* LoRA rank (r=16 or 32), alpha, dropout
* Quantization: 4-bit NF4, double quantization
* Learning rate, scheduler, warmup steps
* Batch size (constrained by T4 16GB VRAM)
* Number of epochs / max steps
* Evaluation frequency

**Conditions to configure:**

1. English code (Condition 1)
2. Multilingual transpiled code (Condition 2)
3. NL text control (Condition 3)
4. Native code (Condition 4, if data available)
5. Regional variants (Water/Fire base models)

**Deliverable:** Config YAML files for each condition, documented and version-controlled.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:native] Validate and quality-review submitted code — is it genuinely native? Meets quality rubric?
Description:
Review all submitted native code files against the quality rubric (#47).

**Review process:**

1. AST parse check (automated — already in CI)
2. Language champion review: is this genuinely native code or translated English?
3. Quality score (1-5 per rubric)
4. Flag and return low-quality submissions with feedback
5. Accept submissions scoring ≥3/5

**Tracking:**

* Accepted vs rejected count per language
* Common rejection reasons
* Average quality score per language

**Deliverable:** Validated set of native code files, quality-scored and ready for packaging.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:training] Run Condition 3 — NL text control QLoRA fine-tune (matched token volume)
Description:
Fine-tune Tiny Aya with multilingual natural language text as a control condition.

**Setup:**

* Base model: Tiny Aya (3.35B params)
* Data: NL text in zh + si + ur from CC-100/OSCAR (volume-matched to transpiled code token count)
* Method: QLoRA 4-bit
* Hardware: Kaggle T4

**What this tests:** Is the improvement from code specifically, or just from more target-language tokens? If NL text performs equally, the code structure doesn't matter — it's just token exposure.

**Deliverables:**

1. Trained LoRA adapter
2. Training logs
3. Comparison: Condition 3 vs Condition 2 isolates code structure from token volume

**Depends on:** NL text datasets (#43-46), training configs (#24)
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Explore a partnership with MLH to amplify the native code collection effort.

3. **How to contribute:**

Title: [research:review] Final review sign-off on all 3 language transpilations before training

Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:data] Build batch transpilation wrapper around Legesher for The Stack processing
Description:
Build a batch processing wrapper around the existing `legesher translate` CLI for processing The Stack at scale.

**Existing capability:** `legesher translate src/ --from en --to zh` handles directories recursively.

**New wrapper needs:**

1. Parallelization (multiprocessing pool for multiple files simultaneously)
2. HuggingFace streaming integration (read from The Stack without downloading full dataset)
3. Error handling with per-file logging (don't stop on individual failures)
4. Progress tracking (files processed, success rate, throughput)
5. Resume capability (skip already-processed files)
6. Output: transpiled files + metadata CSV (original path, language, success/failure, error details)

**Target:** Process 50K-100K files per language at >100 files/sec throughput.

**Reference:** Stress test (CORE-685) showed 156 files/sec on token backend, 133 files/sec on tree-sitter.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:demo] Prepare 5-minute demo/talk for hackathon presentation to mentors
Description:
Prepare the 5-minute demo/talk for the Cohere Labs Phase 2 hackathon wrap-up presentation.

2. Approach: Legesher transpilation + Tiny Aya fine-tuning (60 sec)
3. Data pipeline: The Stack → transpiled code → training (60 sec)
4. Results: key findings from evaluation (90 sec)
5. Native code collection: community participation results (30 sec)
6. Next steps / Phase 3 publication plan (30 sec)

**Deliverables:**

* Slide deck (5-7 slides)
* Demo script with timing
* Backup: pre-recorded demo video in case of technical issues

**Note:** This presentation is considered for Phase 3 (publication) advancement.

Title: [research:training] Run Condition 2 — Multilingual transpiled code QLoRA fine-tune (zh, si, ur) on Tiny Aya base
Description:
Fine-tune Tiny Aya base with multilingual transpiled Python code (all 3 target languages combined).

* Base model: Tiny Aya (3.35B params)
* Data: Transpiled Python code in zh + si + ur (combined, volume-matched to Condition 1)
* Method: QLoRA 4-bit
* Hardware: Kaggle T4

**What this tests:** The core hypothesis — does multilingual code (transpiled) improve target-language reasoning?

**Deliverables:**

1. Trained LoRA adapter
2. Training logs to experiment tracker
3. Training report

**Depends on:** Transpiled datasets (#15, #22), training configs (#24)

Title: [research:review] Review 50-file transpilation samples for Amharic (am) and Urdu (ur)
Description:
Language champions review 50-file samples of Amharic and Urdu transpiled code.

**Same review criteria as Chinese review (CORE-1146) but with additional attention to:**

Day 8 midpoint sync to review first experimental results.

Title: [research:native] Set up code submission repository on GitHub — templates, CI validation, contributor guide
Description:
Create a public GitHub repository for native code submissions.

**Repo structure:**

```
native-code-collection/
├── README.md (contributor guide)
├── CONTRIBUTING.md (submission instructions)
├── LICENSE (Apache 2.0)
├── exercises/ (exercise descriptions per tier)
├── submissions/

│   └── ur/ (Urdu submissions)
├── .github/
│   ├── PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md
```

Title: [research:training] Run Condition 1 — English code QLoRA fine-tune on Tiny Aya base
Description:
Fine-tune Tiny Aya base model with English Python code using QLoRA.

**Setup:**

* Base model: Tiny Aya (3.35B params)
* Data: English Python code from The Stack (volume-matched)
* Method: QLoRA 4-bit (NF4), \~5.4GB VRAM
* Hardware: Kaggle T4

**What this tests:** Replicates the "To Code or Not to Code" finding — does English code improve reasoning?

**Deliverables:**

1. Trained LoRA adapter (saved to HuggingFace)
2. Training logs (loss curves, eval metrics) logged to experiment tracker
3. Training report: total tokens, compute hours, final loss

**Depends on:** English code dataset (#25), training configs (#24)
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:pipeline] Pull Python subset from The Stack via HuggingFace streaming API
Description:
Stream the Python subset from The Stack v1 via HuggingFace Datasets streaming API.

**Steps:**

1. Set up HuggingFace streaming for `bigcode/the-stack` Python subset
2. Stream and save Python files locally (target: 100K-200K files to select from)
3. Track metadata: file size, repo origin, license
4. Implement checkpoint/resume for long-running streaming
5. Estimate total size and validate against available storage

**Note:** Do NOT download the full 6TB dataset — use streaming to pull only the Python subset.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:pipeline] Build filtering pipeline — AST parse, dedup, length filters, license checks
Description:
Build a filtering pipeline to select high-quality Python files from The Stack for transpilation.

**Filters:**

1. **AST parseable:** File must parse as valid Python (reject syntax errors)
2. **Deduplication:** Remove near-duplicates (MinHash or exact hash)
3. **Length filters:** Min 10 lines, max 1000 lines (focus on meaningful code)
4. **License check:** Only permissive licenses (MIT, Apache 2.0, BSD)
5. **Quality heuristics:** Reject auto-generated code, config files, test boilerplate
6. **Encoding:** Must be valid UTF-8

**Output:** Filtered dataset of 50K-100K high-quality Python files ready for transpilation.

**Deliverable:** Filtering script + report on filtering statistics (how many rejected at each stage).
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:milestone] Data handoff sync — verify all 4 training datasets, confirm token counts, green-light training
Description:
Day 5 (Friday March 14) sync to verify all datasets are ready for training.

**Checklist:**

- [ ] Transpiled code datasets (zh, si, ur) — packaged and validated
- [ ] English code dataset — volume-matched, packaged
- [ ] NL text datasets (zh, si, ur) — volume-matched, packaged
- [ ] Native code collection — status update (may not be complete)
- [ ] Language champion sign-off received for all 3 languages
- [ ] Token counts confirmed and matched across conditions

**Decision:** Green-light training to begin on Monday, or identify blockers.

**Deliverable:** Go/no-go decision for each training condition.

Title: [research:native] Package native code as HuggingFace Dataset with metadata (language, exercise tier, contributor ID)

**Format:** Parquet, single dataset with all languages

**Deliverable:** HuggingFace Dataset ready for Condition 4 training (#29).

**Note:** This dataset is independently publishable — the first open-source multilingual native code dataset.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:data] Tokenize NL data using Tiny Aya tokenizer — volume-match to transpiled code token counts
Description:
Tokenize the NL text using the same Tiny Aya tokenizer and match token volume to the transpiled code datasets.

**Steps:**

1. Load Tiny Aya tokenizer
2. Tokenize all cleaned NL text per language
3. Count total tokens per language
4. Trim or sample to exactly match transpiled code token counts per language
5. Verify token distribution is reasonable (not dominated by short/long texts)

**Critical:** Volume matching must be exact (same token count as transpiled code) for the control condition to be valid. The comparison is: "same number of target-language tokens, but NL text instead of code."
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:milestone] Final hackathon sync — review results, decide publication scope
Description:
Day 15 (Monday March 24) final sync before the 5-minute demo/talk.

**Agenda:**

1. Review complete evaluation matrix results
2. Summarize key findings:
   * Does multilingual code improve Tiny Aya? By how much?
   * Does native code differ from transpiled?
   * Regional variant effects?
   * Cross-lingual transfer?
3. Review demo/talk slides and script
4. Decide publication scope for Phase 3:
   * Full paper? Workshop paper? Blog post?
   * Which findings are strong enough to publish?
   * What additional experiments are needed?
5. Celebrate the team's work!

**Deliverable:** Publication decision + polished demo ready for presentation.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:eval] Set up culturally-grounded benchmarks — MultiNRC, AI4Math alongside XNLI/XStoryCloze/TyDi QA
Description:
Add culturally-grounded benchmarks beyond the standard multilingual NLU suite.

**Benchmarks to add:**

* **MultiNRC:** Multi-sentence reading comprehension (tests deeper understanding)
* **AI4Math:** Mathematical reasoning in multiple languages
* Any other culturally-grounded benchmarks available for zh/si/ur

**Steps:**

1. Download and validate MultiNRC and AI4Math for target languages
2. Write evaluation harness integrations (compatible with pipeline from #31)
3. Run on baseline model to establish floor
4. Document any language coverage gaps

**Why:** Standard benchmarks (XNLI, XStoryCloze) test basic NLU. Culturally-grounded benchmarks test whether the model improves on tasks that require deeper language-specific reasoning.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:native] Design submission flow — file format, metadata requirements, consent/licensing
Description:
Design how contributors submit native code files.

**Submission format:**

* Python files (.py) written using Legesher
* Metadata per file: contributor ID (anonymous), language, exercise tier, time spent
* Consent form: permission to use code in research + publish as open dataset

**Licensing:**

* Code submitted under Apache 2.0 or CC-BY-4.0
* Contributors retain copyright
* Clear terms of use for research purposes

**Flow options:**

1. GitHub PR to submission repo (preferred — built-in CI validation)
2. Google Form + file upload (lower barrier)
3. Email submission (fallback)

**Deliverable:** Submission flow document + consent form template + metadata schema.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:training] Prepare English code dataset — pull English Python from The Stack, tokenize, package
Description:
Prepare the English code baseline dataset (Condition 1) that replicates the "To Code or Not to Code" paper's approach.

**Steps:**

1. Pull English Python files from The Stack (same source as transpiled data)
2. Match volume: same number of tokens as the transpiled datasets
3. Tokenize using Tiny Aya tokenizer
4. Package as HuggingFace Dataset (same format as transpiled datasets)
5. Create train/validation splits

**Important:** This must be volume-matched to the transpiled code datasets for fair comparison. Token counts should be equivalent.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:native] Write DEV.to article — "We're building the first multilingual code dataset. Here's how you can help."

Write and publish a [DEV.to](<http://DEV.to>) article to recruit native code contributors.

**Article structure:**

1. Hook: "99% of code uses English keywords. We're changing that."
2. The problem: all open-source code datasets are English-keyword only
3. The research: does multilingual code improve AI models?
4. What we need: developers who speak Chinese, Amharic, or Urdu to write code
5. How to contribute: link to GitHub repo + exercises
6. The impact: first multilingual code dataset, open-source, helping under-resourced languages

**Target audience:** Multilingual developers, especially those who speak Chinese, Amharic, or Urdu.

**Deliverable:** Published [DEV.to](<http://DEV.to>) article with call to action.

Title: [research:eval] Run full evaluation matrix — all conditions x all languages x base vs regional variants
Description:
Run the complete evaluation matrix once all adapters are trained.

**Matrix:**

* Conditions: Baseline, Cond 1 (English), Cond 2 (transpiled), Cond 3 (NL text), Cond 4 (native, if available)
* Languages: zh, si, ur
* Base models: Global, Water (zh), Fire (si, ur)
* Benchmarks: XNLI, XStoryCloze, TyDi QA, MMLU, MultiNRC, AI4Math

**Total evaluations:** \~5 conditions × 3 languages × 2 bases × 6 benchmarks = \~180 runs

**Deliverable:** Complete results spreadsheet with all numbers, ready for analysis (#36, #37).
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:data] Pull multilingual NL text for zh/am/ur from CC-100 or OSCAR
Description:
Pull multilingual natural language text corpora for the NL text control condition (Condition 3).

**Sources (pick best available):**

* CC-100: Common Crawl filtered text in 100+ languages
* OSCAR: Open Super-large Crawled Aggregated corpus
* mC4: Multilingual C4

**Per language (zh, am, ur):**

1. Download text corpus via HuggingFace or direct download
2. Verify language quality (not just language-detected noise)
3. Target volume: enough text to volume-match the transpiled code token counts

**Note:** Amharic may have smaller corpus availability than Chinese/Urdu. Document actual volume available.

**Deliverable:** Raw NL text files for all 3 languages, ready for filtering and tokenization.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:eval] Run baseline evaluation — Tiny Aya base (no fine-tuning) on all benchmarks for zh/si/ur
Description:
Establish baseline numbers by evaluating Tiny Aya base model (no fine-tuning) on all benchmarks.

**Run for each language (zh, si, ur):**

* XNLI
* XStoryCloze
* TyDi QA
* MMLU

**Deliverable:** Baseline results table — this is the floor that all conditions must beat to show improvement.

**Note:** Run on both global base AND regional variants (Water for zh, Fire for si/ur) to establish variant baselines too.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:review] Spot-check model outputs in target languages + write language-specific notes
Description:
During Week 2, language champions spot-check model outputs to provide qualitative feedback.

**Tasks:**

1. Review a sample of model outputs (generations) in each target language
2. Assess: fluency, cultural awareness, reasoning quality
3. Compare outputs across conditions (blind if possible)
4. Write language-specific notes documenting:
   * Strengths and weaknesses per condition
   * Common errors or patterns
   * Qualitative impressions of improvement (or lack thereof)

**Deliverable:** Language-specific notes document per language, ready for inclusion in results summary (#17).
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [core:translation] Round-trip adds/removes lines on 4.1% of files
Description:
## Context

Discovered in CORE-685 stress test. 41/1000 files where en→zh→en round-trip changes the number of lines. Both backends affected equally. Likely a whitespace/newline normalization issue in the translation engine. Need to investigate whether trailing newlines, blank lines, or line endings are being modified during translation.

## Related

* CORE-685
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:eval] Cross-lingual transfer analysis + results visualizations (bar charts, heatmaps, tables)
Description:
Analyze cross-lingual transfer effects and create publication-quality visualizations.

**Cross-lingual transfer tests:**

* Does ur code fine-tuning improve hi performance? (related languages, both on Fire)
* Does zh code improve ja/ko performance? (CJK family, Water variant)
* Does training on one language's code help other languages?

**Visualizations to create:**

1. Bar chart: benchmark scores by condition (grouped by language)
2. Heatmap: condition × language × benchmark scores
3. Radar chart: multi-dimensional performance comparison
4. Line chart: training loss curves across conditions
5. Table: complete results matrix (for paper appendix)

**Tools:** matplotlib, seaborn, or plotly for visualizations

**Deliverable:** Publication-ready figures + cross-lingual transfer analysis writeup.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:training] Set up regional variant experiments — zh→Water, am→Earth, ur→Fire vs Global
Description:
Configure experiments comparing Tiny Aya regional variants vs the global base model.

**Regional variant mapping:**

* Chinese (zh) → Tiny Aya Water (Asia-Pacific + Europe)
* Amharic (am) → Tiny Aya Earth (Africa/West Asia)
* Urdu (ur) → Tiny Aya Fire (South Asia)

**This gives us coverage across all 3 Tiny Aya regional variants.**

**Experiments:**

1. Fine-tune regional variant with target language code vs fine-tuning global base
2. Compare: does a region-specific base model benefit more from target-language code?
3. Cross-lingual transfer: does am code on Earth help other African languages?

**Deliverable:** Configured experiment configs for all 3 language-variant pairs.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:pipeline] Upload final datasets to HuggingFace (public) — write dataset cards with documentation
Description:
Upload all datasets to the shared HuggingFace organization and write comprehensive dataset cards.

**Per dataset card:**

1. Dataset description and motivation
2. How it was created (Legesher transpilation pipeline)
3. Source: The Stack v1 Python subset
4. Languages covered
5. Statistics: file count, token count, average file length
6. Intended use: multilingual code LLM training
7. Limitations and biases
8. License information
9. Citation format

**Datasets to upload:**

* Transpiled code: zh, si, ur (3 datasets)
* English code baseline (1 dataset)
* NL text control (3 datasets)
* Native code (if collected, 1 dataset)

**Note:** These are independently publishable on HuggingFace regardless of experimental results.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:eval] Set up native speaker evaluation protocol — recruit 3-5 raters/language, 50 outputs, Likert
Description:
Design and set up human evaluation protocol using native speakers.

**Protocol:**

1. Select 50 model outputs per language (balanced across conditions)
2. Recruit 3-5 native speakers per language (zh, si, ur)
3. Evaluation criteria (Likert scale 1-5):
   * Fluency: Is the output natural and grammatically correct?
   * Relevance: Does the output address the prompt?
   * Cultural appropriateness: Does it reflect the language's cultural context?
4. Blind evaluation (raters don't know which condition produced the output)
5. Calculate inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa)

**Recruitment:** Through language champions, Code Division/Naamal contacts

**Deliverable:** Evaluation protocol document, recruited raters, evaluation form/spreadsheet.

**Note:** May extend into Phase 3 if rater recruitment takes time.
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research:native] Share early findings / progress update on DEV.to — build momentum, attract more contributors

Write a follow-up [DEV.to](<http://DEV.to>) article sharing progress and early findings from the native code collection.

Title: [research:training] Run Condition 4 — Native code QLoRA fine-tune (if sufficient native code collected)
Description:
Fine-tune Tiny Aya with natively-written multilingual code (code written by developers who think in the target language).

**Setup:**

* Data: Native code collected from community contributors (#56-57)

**What this tests:** The most novel question — does code written by native-language thinkers carry different training signals than mechanically transpiled code?

3. Comparison: Condition 4 vs Condition 2 tests transpiled vs native

Title: [i18n:es] Spanish language pack has ~8% lower round-trip accuracy than other languages
Description:
## Context

Discovered during CORE-685 cross-language stress test. The same 1000 files from The Stack were tested across 5 languages (ar, es, ja, ru, zh). Spanish is a significant outlier.

## Problem

Spanish round-trip accuracy is \~8% lower than all other tested languages:

| Language | Token RT Exact | Tree-Sitter RT Exact |
| -- | -- | -- |
| ar/ja/ru/zh | 93.8% | 95.5% |
| **es** | **85.8%** | **86.7%** |

That's **80 extra mismatches** (142 vs 62 token, 133 vs 45 tree-sitter).

### Additional anomalies

* **Zero tree-sitter errors** (vs 7 for all other languages) — Spanish translations are ASCII, so byte boundaries don't shift to expose the 0x9f bug (CORE-799)
* **7 more backend divergences** (30 vs 23) — suggests more files where token and tree-sitter disagree on Spanish translations
* Some mismatches have `mismatch_first_line=None` — trailing newline detection bug in script (separate issue)

## Likely cause

Translation conflicts in the Spanish language pack — Spanish keywords that collide with common Python identifiers or variable names. For example, if a Spanish keyword maps to a word that also appears as a common variable name in English source code, the reverse translation could pick the wrong one.

## Investigation needed

1. Dump the es language pack keyword/builtin maps and look for English words that are also common Python identifiers
2. Compare the 80 extra mismatch files: diff the es round-trip output against the original to find which specific tokens are being mis-translated
3. Check if the issue is forward translation (en→es introduces ambiguity) or reverse translation (es→en picks wrong token)

## Impact

* Spanish should not be used for Tiny Aya training data until this is resolved or understood
* Other 4 languages (ar, ja, ru, zh) are confirmed safe for dataset generation

## Related

* CORE-685: Stress test that surfaced this
* CORE-1107: Token scope-unaware builtin translation
* CORE-799: Tree-sitter 0x9f errors (not triggered by Spanish)
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [core:token] Token backend translates variable names matching builtins — 1.8% round-trip failure
Description:
## Context

Discovered in CORE-685 stress test. 18/1000 files where TokenTranslator translates identifiers that happen to match builtin names (e.g., variables named `type`, `input`, `list`) causing round-trip mismatch. TreeSitterTranslator handles these correctly. Not a bug per se — the forward translation produces working code — but round-trip is lossy. 16 of these 18 files are also backend divergence cases.

## Options

1. Document as known behavior
2. Add heuristic scope detection to token backend
3. Recommend tree-sitter for precision-critical use

## Related

* CORE-685
* CORE-799
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research] Fix mismatch_first_line bug in stress test script — splitlines() strips trailing newlines
Description:
## Context

Discovered while analyzing CORE-685 cross-language stress test results. The `find_first_diff_line()` function in `research/stress_test_transpiler.py` returns `None` for some round-trip mismatches.

## Problem

`str.splitlines()` strips trailing newlines, so:

* `"hello\n"` → `["hello"]`
* `"hello"` → `["hello"]`

These are different strings but produce identical lists, so the function returns `None` when the only difference is trailing whitespace/newlines.

Observed: 4 out of 5 first AR mismatches have `mismatch_first_line=None`. Several ES mismatches also affected.

## Fix

In `find_first_diff_line()` (line 107 of `research/stress_test_transpiler.py`):

Option A: Use `splitlines(keepends=True)` to preserve line endings
Option B: Add a trailing newline comparison before the line-by-line check

```python
def find_first_diff_line(original: str, result: str) -> int | None:
    orig_lines = original.splitlines(keepends=True)
    result_lines = result.splitlines(keepends=True)
    for i, (a, b) in enumerate(zip(orig_lines, result_lines)):
        if a != b:
            return i + 1
    if len(orig_lines) != len(result_lines):
        return min(len(orig_lines), len(result_lines)) + 1
    return None
```

## Impact

Low — this is a diagnostic improvement, not a translation bug. But accurate mismatch reporting helps debug CORE-1160 and CORE-704.

## Related

* CORE-685: Stress test script
* CORE-1160: Round-trip line count changes
* CORE-704: Spanish pack investigation
Creator: madi@legesher.com

Title: [research] Transpiler stress test — 1000 files from The Stack
Description:
## Goal

Validate Legesher's transpiler can handle real-world Python files at scale before using it to generate multilingual code datasets for the Expedition Tiny Aya research project.

## What

A stress test script (`research/stress_test_transpiler.py`) that:

* Streams 1000 Python files from The Stack (HuggingFace)
* Translates each with both backends (TokenTranslator + TreeSitterTranslator) to Chinese (zh)
* Validates round-trip correctness (en → zh → en)
* Compares backend outputs for divergences
* Produces a comprehensive report

## Results (2026-03-08)

### Single-Language Summary (zh, 1000 files)

| Metric | Value |
| -- | -- |
| Files processed | 1,000 |
| Total time | 48 seconds |
| Token backend success | 100% |
| Token round-trip exact match | 93.8% |
| Tree-Sitter backend success | 99.3% |
| Tree-Sitter round-trip exact match | 95.5% |
| Backend divergences | 23 (2.3%) |
| Tree-Sitter encoding errors | 7 (0.7%) |
| Silent failures | 0 |

### Cross-Language Summary (1000 files × 5 languages)

| Language | Token RT Exact | TS RT Exact | TS Errors | Divergences |
| -- | -- | -- | -- | -- |
| ar (Arabic) | 93.8% | 95.5% | 7 | 23 |
| **es (Spanish)** | **85.8%** | **86.7%** | **0** | **30** |
| ja (Japanese) | 93.8% | 95.5% | 7 | 23 |
| ru (Russian) | 93.8% | 95.5% | 7 | 23 |
| zh (Chinese) | 93.8% | 95.5% | 7 | 23 |

4/5 languages produce identical results. Spanish is a significant outlier (CORE-704).

### Key Findings

1. **Token backend: 100% forward-translation success** — no crashes or errors on any of the 1000 files.
2. **Tree-Sitter backend: 99.3% success** — 7 files failed due to UTF-8 encoding errors on files with non-UTF-8 bytes in string literals (CORE-799). Error is language-dependent — 0 errors with Spanish (ASCII translations).
3. **Round-trip fidelity (Token):** 93.8% exact match. 6.2% mismatch breakdown:
   * 4.1% (41 files): Line count changes — whitespace/newline normalization issue affecting both backends equally (CORE-1160).
   * 1.8% (18 files): Token backend translates variable names matching builtins (e.g., `type`, `input`, `list`) — scope-unaware behavior. Tree-Sitter handles these correctly (CORE-1107).
   * 0.3% (3 files): Other minor differences.
4. **Round-trip fidelity (Tree-Sitter):** 95.5% exact match (higher than Token due to scope awareness).
5. **Backend divergences:** 23 files (2.3%) where Token and Tree-Sitter produce different output. 16 of these overlap with the scope-unaware token translation cases.
6. **Zero silent failures** — every error was caught and reported.
7. **Scale validates**: zh-5000 rates within 0.4% of zh-1000. Memory grows sub-linearly.

### Recommendation

**Token backend is ready for 50K–100K scale dataset generation.** Forward translation is 100% reliable, and the round-trip mismatches are well-understood edge cases that don't affect forward translation quality. Tree-Sitter is more precise but the encoding errors need CORE-799 resolved first for full reliability.

### Follow-up Issues

* CORE-799: Tree-Sitter UTF-8 encoding errors (7 files, 0.7%) — language-dependent
* CORE-1160: Round-trip line count changes (41 files, 4.1%)
* CORE-1107: Token backend scope-unaware builtin translation (18 files, 1.8%)
* CORE-704: Spanish language pack \~8% lower round-trip accuracy
* CORE-696: Stress test script `mismatch_first_line` bug (trailing newline detection)
Creator: madi@legesher.com
